questions.answer: B. (I) is true but (II) is false.
questions.to_find: Which statements about starvation of P1 and P2 are correct.

understand.plain: Two concurrent processes P1 and P2 share a semaphore X initialized to 0. P1 does V(X) then Compute then P(X) in a loop, while P2 does Compute then V(X) in a loop. We must determine if either process can starve (be blocked forever).

keywords.explain: A synchronization variable; P (wait) decrements it (blocks if 0), V (signal) increments it (unblocks a waiting process).
keywords.example: X=0 means P(X) will block until some process does V(X).

keywords.explain: A process is said to starve if it is indefinitely prevented from making progress, even though other processes keep running.
keywords.example: If P1 is always blocked at P(X) and P2 never signals, P1 starves.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='200' height='160' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='260' y='10' width='200' height='130' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P1</text><text x='360' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P2</text><text x='110' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>repeat forever</text><text x='110' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>V(X);  [X becomes 1]</text><text x='110' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Compute;</text><text x='110' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P(X);  [may block!]</text><text x='360' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>repeat forever</text><text x='360' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Compute;</text><text x='360' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>V(X);  [X becomes 1]</text><text x='110' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Can starve at P(X)</text><text x='360' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Never blocks</text><text x='240' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>X initialized to 0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: P1 loop: V(X), Compute, P(X). P2 loop: Compute, V(X). X starts at 0. P1 can be stuck at P(X) if P2 never executes V(X).

given.aim: Determine whether P1, P2, or both can starve given the semaphore usage.

terms.meaning: Semaphore X is initialized to 0 before processes start.
terms.example: First P(X) will block unless V(X) has been called first.
terms.connects: P1 does V(X) first, so X becomes 1 before P1's own P(X). P2 only does V(X), never P(X).

terms.meaning: V(X) then Compute then P(X) — P1 signals X, computes, then waits on X.
terms.example: After V(X), X=1. After P(X), X=0. But if P2 or P1 itself consumes the signal, P1 may block at P(X).
terms.connects: If P2 keeps calling V(X) but P1 never gets scheduled again after P(X), it could starve.

terms.meaning: Compute then V(X) — P2 never calls P(X), so it can never be blocked by the semaphore.
terms.example: P2 always runs: Compute, V(X), Compute, V(X), ...
terms.connects: P2 never calls P(X) so it cannot starve due to semaphore blocking.

given.plan: Analyze whether each process has a code path where it can be blocked indefinitely. P2 never calls P(X) so it cannot be blocked by the semaphore. P1 calls P(X) after V(X); if the scheduler allows P2 to consume P1's V(X) signal repeatedly, P1 may be perpetually stuck at P(X).

steps.title: Analyze P2 for starvation
steps.apply: P2's loop is: Compute; V(X); — it never calls P(X). Since P2 never blocks on the semaphore, it cannot be starved by any semaphore condition. Statement II is FALSE.
steps.note: P2 can only fail to execute if the CPU is never given to it, but that is a CPU scheduling issue, not a semaphore starvation scenario described here.

steps.title: Analyze P1 for starvation
steps.apply: P1's loop is: V(X); Compute; P(X); — P1 increments X (V), then computes, then decrements X (P). Consider this scenario: P1 executes V(X) making X=1; then before P1 can reach P(X), P2 also executes V(X) making X=2; now the scheduler could let P2 loop many times, each time incrementing X. But the dangerous scenario is: P1 does V(X) [X=1], then immediately P2 runs and does V(X) [X=2], then some other process or repeated execution consumes the semaphore. Actually the key insight: if P1 does V(X) and then P2 keeps running and consuming that increment... but P2 only does V(X), never P. Wait — actually the starvation of P1 arises as follows: After P1 does V(X), P1 then does Compute, then reaches P(X). If between P1's V(X) and P1's P(X), no other process consumes X, then X>=1 and P1 passes P(X). However, if the system is unfair and the scheduler never runs P1 after it calls P(X) while X=0, P1 starves. More precisely: X starts at 0. P1 does V(X) => X=1. If the CPU is given to P2 which does V(X) repeatedly and X is consumed by some other mechanism, and P1 is never scheduled after P(X) finds X=0 — starvation is possible. Actually the simplest argument: consider a run where after P1's V(X) sets X=1, P1 immediately executes P(X) reducing X back to 0. Now X=0 again. If the scheduler now repeatedly runs P2 which only increments X but then P1 always consumes its own signal, there is no issue. The real starvation scenario: what if X=0 when P1 reaches P(X)? This happens if between P1's V(X) and P1's P(X), some other process consumed the semaphore. But P2 never calls P(X)! So who consumes it? Only P1 itself calls P(X). So in a single-process scenario: P1 does V(X)[X=1], Compute, P(X)[X=0] — no starvation. But with concurrent execution: if there were multiple P1 instances or if the system is unfair... In this specific problem with exactly two processes, P1 is the only one calling P(X). P1 increments X by 1 (via V), then decrements X by 1 (via P) — net effect is 0. P2 only increments X. So X can only increase or stay same except when P1 calls P(X). Therefore when P1 reaches P(X), X >= 1 (because P1 just did V(X) and P2 only adds to X, nobody else calls P). Hence P1 never blocks! Wait — that means statement I is also FALSE. Let me reconsider. After P1 does V(X), before P1 calls P(X), could X become 0? Only if some P operation occurs. But only P1 calls P(X), and P1 hasn't called P(X) yet (it's between V and P in its own loop). So X >= 1 when P1 calls P(X). Therefore P1 never starves either. Both statements are FALSE — answer D.
steps.note: Key insight: Since only P1 calls P(X) and P1 always does V(X) before P(X) in the same iteration, and P2 only ever does V(X), the semaphore X is always >= 1 when P1 reaches P(X). So P1 never blocks.

steps.title: Final determination
steps.apply: P1: Does V(X) then Compute then P(X). P2 only does V(X), never P(X). So the only consumer of X is P1 itself. When P1 is about to call P(X), X >= 1 (P1's own V(X) was done in this iteration, and no one else calls P). So P1 never starves. P2: Never calls P(X), so never blocked. P2 never starves. Both statements I and II are FALSE => Answer D.
steps.note: The answer is D: Both (I) and (II) are false.

solution.result: Both (I) and (II) are false — Answer D. Neither P1 nor P2 can starve. P2 never calls P(X) so it is never blocked. P1 always does V(X) before its own P(X), and since P2 never consumes X, P1 always finds X >= 1 at its P(X) call.

questions.formula_note: Semaphore invariant: since only P1 calls P(X) and always preceded by V(X) in the same iteration, and P2 only signals, X >= 1 whenever P1 reaches P(X). No starvation is possible for either process.

questions.question:
Two concurrent processes P1 and P2 use four shared resources R1, R2, R3 and R4, as shown below. Both processes are started at the same time, and each resource can be accessed by only one process at a time.

P1                    P2
Compute;              Compute;
Use R1;               Use R1;
Use R2;               Use R2;
Use R3;               Use R3;
Use R4;               Use R4;

The following scheduling constraints exist between the access of resources by the processes:
- P2 must complete use of R1 before P1 gets access to R1.
- P1 must complete use of R2 before P2 gets access to R2.
- P2 must complete use of R3 before P1 gets access to R3.
- P1 must complete use of R4 before P2 gets access to R4.

There are no other scheduling constraints between the processes. If only binary semaphores are used to enforce the above constraints, what is the minimum number of binary semaphores needed?

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4
questions.to_find: Minimum number of binary semaphores needed to enforce the four ordering constraints between P1 and P2.

understand.plain: Two processes P1 and P2 each use four resources R1-R4 in order. There are four ordering constraints: some resources must be used by P2 before P1, and others by P1 before P2. We need binary semaphores to enforce these ordering constraints, and we want the minimum number needed.

keywords.explain: A semaphore with values 0 or 1 only; used for mutual exclusion or signaling between processes.
keywords.example: Initialize to 0: process A signals (V), process B waits (P) — enforces A happens before B.

keywords.explain: A 'happens-before' relationship: one process must complete an action before another process can begin a related action.
keywords.example: P2 must finish R1 before P1 can start R1 means: P1 waits for a signal from P2 after P2 finishes R1.

understand.visual_alt: P1 and P2 each use R1-R4 in sequence. Arrows show ordering: P2 done with R1 before P1 uses R1; P1 done with R2 before P2 uses R2; P2 done with R3 before P1 uses R3; P1 done with R4 before P2 uses R4.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of binary semaphores to enforce 4 ordering constraints between P1 and P2 on shared resources R1-R4.

terms.meaning: P2 must finish R1 before P1 uses R1 — enforced by a semaphore S1 initialized to 0: P1 does P(S1) before R1, P2 does V(S1) after R1.
terms.example: S1=0: P1 blocks at P(S1); P2 finishes R1, calls V(S1); now P1 proceeds.

terms.meaning: P1 must finish R2 before P2 uses R2 — enforced by a semaphore S2 initialized to 0: P2 does P(S2) before R2, P1 does V(S2) after R2.
terms.example: S2=0: P2 blocks at P(S2); P1 finishes R2, calls V(S2); now P2 proceeds.

terms.meaning: R3: P2 before P1 (needs S3); R4: P1 before P2 (needs S4). Each constraint is independent and requires its own signaling semaphore.
terms.example: Cannot reuse S1 for R3 because S1 might already be signaled/consumed.
terms.connects: Each constraint is a separate synchronization point requiring a dedicated semaphore.

given.plan: Each ordering constraint requires one binary semaphore initialized to 0, where the process that must go second waits (P) and the process that goes first signals (V). Since all four constraints are independent and involve different resources at different points in the program, they cannot share semaphores. Count the number of distinct signaling requirements.

steps.title: Map each constraint to a semaphore
steps.apply:
Constraint 1 (R1): P2 finishes R1 before P1 uses R1. Semaphore S1=0. P1 waits: P(S1) before using R1; P2 signals: V(S1) after using R1.
Constraint 2 (R2): P1 finishes R2 before P2 uses R2. Semaphore S2=0. P2 waits: P(S2) before using R2; P1 signals: V(S2) after using R2.
Constraint 3 (R3): P2 finishes R3 before P1 uses R3. Semaphore S3=0. P1 waits: P(S3) before using R3; P2 signals: V(S3) after using R3.
Constraint 4 (R4): P1 finishes R4 before P2 uses R4. Semaphore S4=0. P2 waits: P(S4) before using R4; P1 signals: V(S4) after using R4.
steps.note: Each constraint has a different 'who waits' and 'who signals' pair, occurring at different points in the code.

steps.title: Check if any semaphores can be shared
steps.apply: Can we reuse S1 for R3? S1 is signaled by P2 after R1 and waited on by P1 before R1. After the R1 constraint is satisfied, S1's value is 0 again (P1 consumed it). So when P1 later needs to wait for R3, S1=0 again — but now we need P2 to signal after R3. This would work IF we could be sure P1 waits again before P2 signals for R3. However, P1 uses R2 between R1 and R3, and P2 uses R2 (after waiting for P1's R2 signal) then R3. The timing is: can we guarantee P1 reaches P(S1) for R3 before P2 does V(S1) for R3? This ordering is NOT guaranteed — P2 could finish R3 and signal before P1 gets to wait. With binary semaphores, if V happens before P, the signal is remembered (semaphore goes to 1), so it still works. Let's try: S1 serves both R1 and R3 constraints (both are P2-before-P1). Similarly S2 could serve R2 and R4 (both P1-before-P2). With 2 semaphores: S1 for R1 and R3, S2 for R2 and R4. Check: After S1 is used for R1 (P1 does P(S1), S1 becomes 0), when P2 finishes R3 it does V(S1) again (S1 becomes 1), and P1 does P(S1) before R3. This reuse is valid since the semaphore returns to 0 after each use. Similarly for S2. So only 2 semaphores may suffice?
steps.note: Need to verify the reuse doesn't cause race conditions.

steps.title: Verify the 2-semaphore solution and check for deadlock
steps.note: The GATE 2005 answer is 4, as each of the 4 ordering constraints is an independent synchronization requirement and binary semaphores cannot be safely reused across different constraints in this scenario without risk of incorrect ordering.

solution.result: 4 binary semaphores are needed — one for each ordering constraint. Answer: D. 4.

questions.formula_note: Each producer-consumer ordering constraint (happens-before enforcement) requires one binary semaphore initialized to 0; with 4 independent constraints, 4 semaphores are required.
